cards.title: The one mandatory job of creative
cards.front: Of all the things an image could do, which one is non-negotiable?
cards.back: Your creative MUST reflect your hook. This is the #1 of the Six Important Aspects and the only one that always must exist. If the image isn't conveying the hook, you're just catching eyeballs and generating unqualified clicks.
cards.example: Hook 'work/life balance' → image of a gauge with work vs. life on it.

cards.title: How long you have on the scroll
cards.front: How many seconds does a scroller actually look at your image?
cards.back: 2-3 seconds. That's all the attention an image gets. Keep the creative simple and make sure it clearly portrays the message in that window.

cards.title: Six Important Aspects of creative
cards.front: What six things should your creative consider — and which one is mandatory?
cards.back:
Six Important Aspects Your Creative Should Have (not all need to appear, but #1 always must):
- 1. Reflect your hook (MANDATORY)
- 2. Tell a story about how the offer changes their life (carousels are great for this)
- 3. Display the product (if physical/demonstrable; works best down-funnel)
- 4. Stand out in the newsfeed (contrasting colors, small video, moving GIFs — flashy but on brand)
- 5. Be on brand (consistent fonts/colors for recognition)
- 6. Play off emotions already associated with an image (icons people have a relationship with)
cards.example: Low iPhone battery or low bank account images to convey scarcity (#6).

cards.title: Why a cute puppy ad fails
cards.front: Why does an unrelated puppy or pretty-woman image hurt your ad?
cards.back: Eyeball-bait imagery only grabs attention and generates unqualified clicks from people who don't care what's behind it. Ad scent breaks the moment they land. If the image isn't conveying the hook, you're just catching eyeballs.
cards.example: An MLM ad used a stock cat at a keyboard with no relation to network marketing — costly, bad for brand, magnet for unqualified clicks.

cards.title: Use literal imagery of the hook keyword
cards.front: What kind of image repeatedly beats clever concepts?
cards.back: Simple, literal images of the hook keyword consistently outperform. Take the biggest keyword in the hook and show it literally — then optionally enhance as a GIF.
cards.example: 'Don't bottle up your emotions' = sad bottles; 'not all wounds are visible' = bandages; 'build it for you' = ninjas building the app; 'work/life balance' = a gauge with the needle moving.

cards.title: Color discipline for ads that pop
cards.front: How many colors should a Facebook ad use to stand out?
cards.back: Use only a few complementary or contrasting colors. Don't add ten different colors or it gets busy. Contrast helps the image stand out against the newsfeed background.

cards.title: Font limit for readability
cards.front: How many font families belong on one ad image?
cards.back: Use only one or two font families per image. Too many fonts hurts readability. (The hard-to-read Amy Porterfield example failed because of too many fonts, not the black background.)

cards.title: Put hook text and CTA on the image
cards.front: Most people skip your ad copy entirely — so where do the key words go?
cards.back: Place a few of the most important hook words (a line of copy or a CTA button) directly on the image itself, because many people skip the ad copy and look straight at the image. Adding text onto a carousel image also helps it stand out.

cards.title: What images clash with the newsfeed
cards.front: Which visual qualities make an image clash with Facebook's UI and grab attention?
cards.back:
Images that clash with Facebook's white/light-blue scheme catch attention:
- Solid black or bright backgrounds
- Black-and-white images
- Close-ups of faces

cards.title: Standard vs. Like-ad image size
cards.front: What's the unusual image size for a Like ad versus the standard?
cards.back: Recommended Like-ad image size is an unusually wide 1200 x 444 px — not the standard, more common 1200 x 628 px. If you don't supply a Like-ad image, Facebook defaults to your cover photo.

cards.title: Designed images beat photographic stock
cards.front: Why does photo-style stock imagery get buried in the feed?
cards.back: Photographic stock blends in with friends'/family's photos and gets buried. Prefer designed/branded images with on-image text to stand out.
cards.example: Kabbage's carousel used stock photos; DM's version of the same concept used their cartoon brand style with on-image text and stood out more.

cards.title: Keyword-to-image extraction process
cards.front: How do you turn a hook into the right image without guessing?
cards.back:
Keyword-to-image extraction process:
- 1. Write the hook
- 2. Pull the biggest keyword(s) out of it (e.g. grade, stack, bottle, build, rock star, guess)
- 3. Think about what people associate with that keyword (grade → report card; stack → stacks of pancakes)
- 4. Type the keyword into Google or Dreamstime and see what comes up first
Google works best — its top images are the ones people clicked most for that keyword, so let Google's dataset tell you the associated image rather than guessing.

cards.title: Direct the subject's gaze
cards.front: When a person is in your image, where should they be looking?
cards.back: Direct the subject's gaze at the viewer or at the CTA — viewers follow the subject's gaze. If a person looks elsewhere, viewers look there too, so point the gaze at whatever you want clicked.
cards.example: The Jimmy Buffett ad had him looking directly at the viewer.

cards.title: Match the ad to the landing page (ad scent)
cards.front: Why should your ad design mirror the page it sends people to?
cards.back: Make ad creative congruent with the landing destination — the landing page's design elements should match the ad for instant brand connection (ad scent). DM keeps ads similar to the featured images on their blog so viewers instantly recognize the brand.

cards.title: Show physical products in a non-boring way
cards.front: How do you keep a physical product shot from being a flat, ignorable image?
cards.back: Add an eye-catching element rather than a plain product shot. Some markets (e.g. beauty) require really high-quality design.
cards.example: Makeup brush with pink blush coming off it; Survival Life waterproof-lighter video with rain falling while it stays lit, a penny, and a CTA; Wag retargeting ad using dogs + '50% off your first walk' baked into the image.

cards.title: Recreate a familiar interface
cards.front: What's a fast, high-converting trick that mimics tools your audience uses daily?
cards.back: Recreate a familiar interface/platform the audience uses every day so it stops the scroll through recognition.
cards.example: The 60-Second Blog ad (one of DM's highest-converting ever) made the image look like WordPress + a clock for '60 seconds' and took ~20 min to design. A Messenger ad was mocked up to look like a Facebook-style message conversation.

cards.title: Borrow a meme format for your niche
cards.front: How can a culturally familiar meme make your ad highly shareable?
cards.back: Borrow a culturally familiar meme/format and apply it to your niche — relatability drives shares. Pattern interrupt also works: arrange product/elements in an unexpected pattern that makes the brain curious.
cards.example: SamCart used the 'what we're told it's like vs. what it's really like' meme applied to sales funnels — the overwhelmed audience could relate.

cards.title: A custom image with no marketing thought
cards.front: Is a custom-designed image automatically better than a stock cat?
cards.back: No. A custom image with no marketing thought is as bad as a stock cat. If the graph doesn't match the copy and the ad gives no reason to care, it blends into the newsfeed. Go through the thought process of conveying the message in the image.
cards.example: The 'no-hook bland-graph' agency ad ('here's how we help our clients generate a strong ROI...') gave no reason to care and the graph was meaningless.

cards.title: Ad Creative Checklist — 6-step designer process
cards.front: What 6 steps walk a creative from hook to finished, on-brand image?
cards.back:
Ad Creative Checklist (6-step designer thought process):
- 1. Create a powerful message & conceptualize images (research hook, Google, competitors; identify benefits, target, end result, emotions)
- 2. Sketch ideas before laying out design elements (don't worry about colors/fonts yet; ensure all marketing elements are present)
- 3. Use complementary or contrasting colors (only a few, not ten)
- 4. Choose your fonts (only one or two families)
- 5. Add text and a CTA to your image (people skip copy, go to image)
- 6. Make sure your image is congruent with your brand (match the landing destination)

cards.title: The five ad format options
cards.front: What are the five Facebook ad format options?
cards.back: Five Format options: Carousel, Single Image, Single Video, Slideshow, Canvas. When sourcing images you have three choices: upload a new image, reuse a previously uploaded image, or use Facebook's free stock images (follow the Recommended Image Specs).

cards.title: Build a Single Image ad
cards.front: What are the steps to build and place a Single Image ad?
cards.back:
Steps to build a Single Image ad:
- After the ad set, click Continue to the ad creation page
- In Format, choose one of the five formats (Single Image here)
- Under Images, upload new / reuse / or use free stock (follow Recommended Image Specs)
- Under Page and Links, select your Facebook page; add an Instagram account if you have one
- Enter Website URL and ad copy
- Add a Call to Action button (always use it)
- Click Review Order, then the green Place Order button
- Click the blue Continue button to finish
- After confirmation, create a new ad in the same window to test different images or copy

cards.title: Spin up variations from the confirmation window
cards.front: What's the fast path to multiple creative variations in one campaign?
cards.back: After confirming an ad, reuse the same order-confirmation window to spin up additional ads testing different images or copy. It's the fast path to multiple creative variations in one campaign.

cards.title: Add captions to video ads
cards.front: Why control your own video captions instead of auto-generating them?
cards.back: In Power Editor under Ads → Video Captions you can auto-generate, upload an SRT file, or use no captions. Captions are recommended because sound-off viewers can still read the message. Don't rely on auto-generation — strong regional accents cause transcription errors; creating the text yourself lets you control size and background color so it stands out.

cards.title: 7 Power Questions — Q4: Pre-expose to emotion
cards.front: How do you make someone feel the buying emotion before you even make the offer?
cards.back:
7 Power Questions — Q4: Pre-expose the audience to an emotional stimulus.
- 1. Identify the desired emotional stimulus: how do you want them to feel in order to buy?
- 2. Pre-expose them to a concept that evokes that emotion BEFORE the offer
- 3. The trigger concept can be completely unrelated to the product — it works purely by priming the emotion (why perfume ads 'sell sex')
cards.example: DigitalMarketer's low-battery ad showed an iPhone ~10% red sliver to transfer nervous urgency to an expiring early-bird sale.

cards.title: The Valentine Street pre-suasion experiment
cards.front: How did one street name dramatically change whether strangers would help?
cards.back: Cialdini's Valentine Street pre-suasion experiment: Control — a woman asks a man to help retrieve her phone from 'ruffians'; most decline. Test — a first woman asks directions to 'Valentine Street' (romance concept), then a second immediately asks for help; willingness jumped dramatically. Re-test with 'Main Street' (unlinked concept) reverted to the low control level. Merely pre-exposing to a concept linked to the desired emotion changed behavior.

cards.title: 7 Power Questions — Q5: Tap associations & nostalgia
cards.front: Beyond emotion, what deeper layer can you attach your offer to?
cards.back:
7 Power Questions — Q5: Tap mental links, associations, and nostalgia. Where Q4 targets emotion, Q5 targets memory and identity (thought occurs by association).
- 1. Ask what nostalgia, metaphor, shared experience, or memory can be positively associated with the offer
- 2. Tap a whole group's shared memories/feelings, then attach the product
- 3. Use metaphors and imagery (even subtle background images) that harken to a memory or desired future
cards.example: Coca-Cola sold 'brown sugar water' for decades by associating it with family, friends, and good times.

cards.title: STEPPS — viral content principles
cards.front: What six principles raise the odds your video gets shared?
cards.back:
STEPPS (Jonah Berger, Contagious) — six principles that raise share probability:
- Social Currency — sharing makes people look in-the-know
- Triggers — sights/sounds that remind us of related products
- Emotion — high-arousal emotion (awe, anger, anxiety) goes viral
- Public — built to be shared by everyone, not exclusive
- Practical Value — useful content that improves lives
- Stories — beginning, middle, end matches how we remember
Virality is science, not luck — these raise probability, not guarantees.
cards.example: Julius Dein grew 0 → 6M+ Facebook followers in 1 year with funny, emotional, social-currency-loaded prank videos.

cards.title: Designer brief / Google-research workflow
cards.front: How do you brief a designer so you get a converting ad, not just a pretty one?
cards.back:
Designer brief / Google-research workflow:
- 1. Google the concept and screenshot images you like into a document
- 2. Do rough drawings so the designer doesn't just clone the found image
- 3. Use the Ad Grid (Perpetual Traffic ep. 33) to map avatars × hooks; per hook generate ~3-5 image ideas
- 4. Always have the designer create one 'wildcard' of her own
- 5. Send the doc, get a first draft
- 6. Walk through every element live so she understands your vision
- 7. Iterate — sometimes 3-5 revisions, sometimes perfect first try

cards.title: Designers are not marketers
cards.front: Why can't you just hand off creative to a designer and walk away?
cards.back: Designers make pretty pictures, not necessarily high-converting ones — you must supply the marketing thinking. Don't tell the designer what colors/fonts to use (that's their strength); your job is to ensure all marketing elements are present. Outsource via Design Pickle (unlimited but can be expensive) or hire in-house (the best thing that happened to DM's ads).

cards.title: Continuously study ads
cards.front: Why is creative never a 'set it and forget it' system?
cards.back: Creative is not a one-time system. Use AdEspresso's Facebook ad library (study almost daily); screenshot ads in your own feed and circulate good/bad examples with the team (DM keeps a Slack room sharing ads daily to educate designers). Avoid relying on Snagit or Fiverr for serious ad creative.

cards.title: Reuse the Post ID to consolidate social proof
cards.front: When you duplicate a winning ad, why do its likes and comments vanish?
cards.back: Every ad has both an Ad ID and a Post ID; the Post ID refers to the content inside. Two ads with identical creative still get unique Post IDs and are treated as separate posts. Nine duplications of a winning ad set create nine new posts (ten total), thinning social proof. Solution: use the SAME Post ID (the original's) in all duplicates — the Ad ID differs per ad, but the shared Post ID collects all comments/likes/shares in one place, and Facebook rewards favorable reactions with more impressions at lower cost.

cards.title: Battery-scarcity retargeting sequence
cards.front: How do you escalate scarcity to retargeted visitors using a battery image?
cards.back:
Battery-scarcity retargeting sequence (visited the site but didn't buy, as a price increase approached):
- Ad 1 (battery icon GREEN, mild scarcity): 'We're excited you visited the site, we're sad you didn't purchase, but there's still time. Batteries don't last forever, and neither will our 50 percent off deal.'
- Ad 2 (one week later, same audience, battery ABOUT TO DIE, strong scarcity): same message, escalated battery image conveying the deal is nearly gone.
